blocks.text: To: Pamela Warren, Project Engineer; Jeffrey Perez, Design Technician
Subject: Kickoff, Stormwater Management Plan for {{fact:f:E-2004-001.client}}

The agreement is signed and we are under way as of {{fact:f:E-2004-001.start}}. What we owe is a sealed plan set with the stormwater report behind it, transmitted to the reviewing authority, plus written answers to the first round of comments. The engagement letter is in the job folder. Read the scope paragraph in it before you open a drawing. It is a lump sum, and the scope is the fence around it.

Nothing gets drawn this month. The first weeks are for finding out what is actually on the ground and what the ordinance is going to make us do about it. I would rather spend two weeks on that now than move a detention pond in the fall.

items: Site reconnaissance. Mine. I will walk the property next week and photograph the outfall, the receiving ditch, and both culverts shown on the survey. The survey is clean, which is what bothers me about it. Anything that does not match, I will mark on a print and put in the folder.

Hydrology. Pamela Warren has the drainage area delineation and the pre-development and post-development runoff computations, for the design storms the ordinance names. Assumptions go in the report where a reviewer can find them, not on the back of a calc sheet.
Conveyance and detention. Pamela Warren sizes the pipe, the pond, and the outlet structure, and not until the reconnaissance is back. We are not sizing anything off the survey alone.
Grading and drainage sheets. Mine. I do not start the sheets until the pond has a volume on it.

Details and quantities. Jeffrey Perez takes the detail sheet and the takeoff. The details wait on the outlet structure and the takeoff waits on the grading, so his early time goes to the erosion and sedimentation control sheets.
Erosion and sedimentation control. Jeffrey Perez to start from our standard sheets and adjust once we know where the limits of disturbance sit.
Submittal and comments. Pamela Warren assembles the package and tracks the comment responses.
Site visit next week. Jeffrey Perez to come out for a half day and see the ground before he details anything on it.

Pamela Warren to telephone David Dyer and ask for the property file his office holds: the old grading permit, whatever exists on the culverts, and the geotechnical report if one was ever done. If Johnson PLC does not have it, we need to know that now and not in September.

David Dyer has asked for a briefing at his office next month. Pamela Warren and I will put a short deck together after the reconnaissance and the delineation are done. Nothing goes in it that we cannot show a source for.
Job meeting in this office Friday mornings, fifteen minutes, standing.
The lettered folder is set up. Everything goes in it. Nothing lives on anybody's desk.

blocks.text: One more thing. The receiving ditch is downstream of a property we do not control. If it turns out to be silted in, that is not our design problem, but it is our schedule problem, and I want it written down the week we see it rather than the week the reviewer asks. Put the note in the file when you see the condition, not when somebody makes it an issue.

On the schedule generally: five months sounds like a long time and it is not. Two of those months belong to the reviewing authority and we do not get to spend them. What we actually have is the front end, and the front end is where a wrong assumption is cheap. Anything that looks off to either of you, say so in the Friday meeting while it still costs an hour to fix.
